planting.note: Direct sow in fall for spring germination; small-headed blazing star grows naturally in dry, rocky, calcareous soils of the Cumberland Plateau and surrounding Appalachian region, in open glades and rock outcrops in full sun.

directions: Plant in dry, rocky, calcareous or slightly acidic, well-drained soil in full sun; small-headed blazing star is a distinctly more slender, more narrowly adapted liatris than the widely cultivated Liatris spicata, native to the open rocky glades and dry, calcareous slopes of the Cumberland Plateau of Tennessee and surrounding areas. The small flower heads give it a more airy, delicate appearance than the more robust blazing stars, and it blooms in late summer with the typical rose-purple liatris color. Like all Liatris species, the flowers open from the top of the spike downward, and it serves as an important late-season nectar source for monarch butterflies and native bees during their fall migration period. An excellent, drought-tolerant species for dry, rocky, calcareous garden soils.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall or early spring in dry, sandy, strongly acidic soil in full sun; Chapman's blazing star is a Coastal Plain endemic growing in dry, sandy pine flatwoods and scrub in Florida and the immediately surrounding states. It requires the xeric, nutrient-poor conditions of the longleaf pine ecosystem.

directions: Plant in dry, sandy, strongly acidic, infertile, well-drained soil in full sun; Chapman's blazing star is a Florida-centered Coastal Plain endemic of dry sandy pine flatwoods and scrub habitats, one of the most drought-tolerant blazing stars adapted to the extreme xeric soils of the longleaf pine ecosystem. The vivid rose-purple flower spikes in summer are as ornamentally attractive as those of the more widely grown liatris species and especially valuable in zone 9a gardens where many temperate liatris species struggle in summer heat. The flowers are critically important late-summer and fall nectar sources for monarch butterflies and numerous native bees in the Gulf South. Plant in dry, infertile, sandy soils in naturalistic pine flatwoods or xeric native plant gardens.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall for spring germination or direct sow in early spring; paleleaf sunflower prefers moderately dry to moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun, growing naturally at woodland edges, dry upland forest margins, and disturbed clearings across the eastern US.

directions: Plant in moderately dry to moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; paleleaf woodland sunflower is one of the most shade-tolerant native sunflowers, growing naturally at the margins of woodlands, dry upland forest edges, and open clearings where it receives dappled to partial shade. The pale green undersides of the leaves distinguish it from similar species. Bright yellow daisy flowers in late summer provide excellent late-season pollinator value — the seeds are important food for goldfinches and other seed-eating birds. As a woodland-edge sunflower, it is particularly valuable for the partial-shade garden where most sunflowers would not bloom reliably. Spreads by rhizomes to form modest colonies without being overly aggressive. A natural companion for native asters and goldenrods in late-season partial-shade borders.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall for spring germination in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; hairy sunflower grows naturally in dry to moderately moist, open and partially wooded habitats across the eastern and central US.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile to lean soil in full sun to partial shade; hairy sunflower is a stiffly hairy, rough-textured native perennial sunflower named for the dense, bristly hairs that cover the stems and leaves. It grows in a wide range of open to partially wooded habitats, tolerating more shade than most sunflowers while still blooming reliably. The bright yellow daisy flowers in late summer and fall are excellent late-season nectar sources for native bees and the seeds are consumed by goldfinches and other seed-eating birds. The rough, hairy foliage is notable deer-resistant compared to the smoother-leaved sunflower species. Spreads gradually by rhizomes to form modest colonies. A reliable, low-maintenance native sunflower for the back of a mixed border or at a woodland edge in zones 4–9.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall for spring germination in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun; large-flowered black-eyed Susan is native to dry, open pine woodlands and prairies of the Gulf Coastal Plain from Texas to Alabama.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile to lean soil in full sun; large-flowered black-eyed Susan is a Gulf Coastal Plain native Rudbeckia producing some of the largest individual flower heads in the genus — bright yellow ray flowers surrounding a dark brown central cone on tall, leafy stems. The plant is a hardy, long-lived perennial adapted to the hot, dry summers and mild winters of the Gulf South, making it better suited to zone 9a than the more commonly planted R. hirta (which often behaves as a short-lived biennial in the South's climate). The showy flower heads persist through summer and into fall, providing extended seasonal color and late-season pollen for native bees. The seed heads that follow are consumed by goldfinches. An excellent, low-water native perennial for dry, sunny, Southern gardens.
